sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A behavioral medicine clinic studied whether personality traits were associated with completion of a 6-week physical-therapy program after knee injury. Patients completed a Big Five inventory at intake. Researchers divided them into lower, middle, and higher conscientiousness groups and recorded the percentage completing at least 90% of prescribed exercises. Completion rates were 44%, 63%, and 79%, respectively. Patients in the high-conscientiousness group were also most likely to use calendars and reminders. The clinic did not assign people to personality groups; it observed existing differences.
questions.stem: Which personality trait in the passage was associated with the highest adherence to therapy exercises?

explanations.B: Correct. The passage directly states that exercise-completion rates increased across conscientiousness groups, reaching 79% in the highest-conscientiousness group. That makes conscientiousness the trait most strongly linked to adherence in this study.
explanations.A: Openness is not the variable described in the completion pattern.
explanations.C: Neuroticism was not the personality dimension tied to the highest completion rate in the passage.
explanations.D: Agreeableness was not the factor organized into low, middle, and high groups here.

questions.stem: The use of calendars and reminders most likely reflects which mechanism?

choices.D: Highly conscientious individuals often use organized self-regulation strategies
choices.B: Patients with reminders no longer need motivation
choices.C: The clinic manipulated personality through calendars

explanations.D: Correct. Conscientiousness is associated with planning, orderliness, and dependable behavior. The reported use of calendars and reminders fits that self-regulatory style and provides a plausible pathway for better adherence.
explanations.A: Personality may influence behavior, but it does not directly cause the biological healing process described here.
explanations.B: Reminders can support adherence, but they do not eliminate the need for motivation or effort.
explanations.C: The study observed preexisting trait differences; it did not manipulate personality.

questions.stem: Why should the clinic be cautious about saying conscientiousness caused better recovery?

choices.A: Patients were not randomly assigned to different levels of conscientiousness
choices.B: The study used exercise completion as an outcome
choices.D: Completion rates were expressed as percentages

explanations.A: Correct. Because conscientiousness was measured rather than manipulated, other characteristics correlated with conscientiousness could partly explain adherence differences. The study supports association, not definitive causation.
explanations.B: Using adherence as an outcome does not create the main limitation.
explanations.C: Studying knee injuries does not by itself weaken causal inference.
explanations.D: Reporting percentages is not the reason the causal claim should be limited.

questions.stem: A clinician applying this study would most reasonably do what?

choices.C: Offer extra planning tools to patients who seem less naturally organized
choices.A: Assume lower-adherence patients are biologically unable to improve
choices.B: Stop giving exercise instructions to highly conscientious patients
choices.D: Use personality labels as exact predictions for every patient

explanations.C: Correct. If organized self-management strategies help explain adherence, then providing structure to patients who need it could improve completion even without changing their personality. That is a practical and cautious use of the findings.
explanations.A: The study does not suggest that improvement is impossible for lower-adherence patients.
explanations.B: Highly conscientious patients still need exercise instructions and clinical guidance.
explanations.D: Personality traits predict tendencies, not guaranteed outcomes for every individual.

questions.stem: Which additional finding would most strengthen the proposed mechanism?

choices.B: Within each conscientiousness group, patients who used more reminders completed more exercises
choices.A: Patients with higher conscientiousness were slightly older on average
choices.C: The clinic served people from several neighborhoods
choices.D: All patients completed the same personality inventory

explanations.B: Correct. If reminder use predicts completion even within conscientiousness groups, that would support the idea that planning tools are part of the pathway linking conscientiousness to adherence rather than just an unrelated detail.
explanations.A: Age differences could matter, but they do not directly test the reminder-based mechanism.
explanations.C: Serving several neighborhoods affects sample diversity more than the explanatory pathway.
explanations.D: Using the same inventory standardizes measurement but does not test the mechanism.

questions.passage: A college counseling center screened students during the first month of the semester. Students were grouped by weekly sleep duration and average number of major deadlines due each week. Those sleeping fewer than 6 hours while managing at least three major deadlines had the highest mean anxiety score, 16 on a 0–21 screening scale. Students sleeping more than 7 hours with one or fewer major deadlines had the lowest mean score, 7. Students in high-stress groups were also more likely to report irritability, racing thoughts, and difficulty concentrating. The screening tool is useful for identifying possible anxiety but does not by itself establish a diagnosis.
questions.stem: What is the most accurate interpretation of the counseling center’s screening results?

choices.A: Higher workload combined with shorter sleep was associated with higher anxiety-screen scores
choices.B: Short sleep proved that students had generalized anxiety disorder
choices.C: Deadlines were unrelated to anxiety scores
choices.D: The scale established final psychiatric diagnoses

explanations.A: Correct. The passage reports the highest scores among students with both short sleep and many deadlines, and the lowest scores among students with more sleep and fewer deadlines. It explicitly frames the tool as a screening instrument rather than a diagnostic one.
explanations.B: A screening score can indicate elevated symptoms, but it does not by itself confirm a formal diagnosis.
explanations.C: Deadline load is part of the grouping structure and is clearly related to the score pattern.
explanations.D: The passage states that screening alone does not establish diagnosis.

questions.stem: Why is it important that the instrument is described as a screening tool?

choices.D: Positive screens identify risk and symptoms but require further evaluation for diagnosis
choices.A: Screening tools are useless in counseling settings
choices.B: A screening score proves the symptoms are temporary
choices.C: The tool measures only sleep and not anxiety

explanations.D: Correct. Screening instruments are designed to flag people who may need closer follow-up. They can identify symptom burden, but diagnosis requires broader clinical assessment, duration criteria, and functional context.
explanations.A: Screening tools can be very useful for triage and outreach, so calling them useless is incorrect.
explanations.B: A high or low score does not prove symptoms will be temporary.
explanations.C: The tool measures anxiety-related symptoms; sleep was a separate grouping variable.

questions.stem: Racing thoughts and difficulty concentrating are best interpreted as what?

choices.C: Symptoms that can be associated with elevated anxiety
choices.A: Evidence that the students were malingering
choices.B: A sign that the students had a personality disorder

explanations.C: Correct. The passage lists these complaints in the higher-stress groups as part of their symptom profile. Such complaints commonly accompany elevated anxiety and stress, which is how they are being used here.
explanations.A: Nothing in the passage suggests intentional fabrication of symptoms.
explanations.B: The study is about anxiety screening, not personality-disorder diagnosis.
explanations.D: Racing thoughts and concentration difficulty do not by themselves prove psychosis.

questions.stem: A campus intervention based on these results would most likely prioritize which students?

choices.B: Students with multiple deadlines and chronically short sleep
choices.A: Students who report zero deadlines and long sleep
choices.C: Only varsity athletes regardless of symptoms
choices.D: Students selected solely by year in school

explanations.B: Correct. The highest screening scores occurred in the students juggling heavy deadline loads while sleeping the least. A prevention or outreach effort would logically prioritize the group showing the greatest symptom burden.
explanations.A: The lowest-risk group would not be the top priority for limited counseling resources.
explanations.C: Athlete status was not the organizing variable in the results.
explanations.D: Year in school is not the main risk pattern described in the passage.

questions.stem: Which statement reflects the main methodological limitation of the screening findings?

choices.D: The data show associations but cannot prove that poor sleep or deadlines caused the anxiety symptoms
choices.A: The study cannot compare students because it used a 0–21 scale
choices.B: The passage is invalid because it used symptom reports
choices.C: The findings are uninterpretable unless every student has exactly the same major

explanations.D: Correct. The observed groups differ on naturally occurring stressors and habits, so the results support association rather than causation. Other factors such as finances, prior mental-health history, or social support could also shape screening scores.
explanations.A: Using a common numerical scale does not invalidate comparison.
explanations.B: Self-report symptoms are often part of legitimate screening methods, even though they have limitations.
explanations.C: Students need not have the same major for the association between sleep, workload, and symptoms to be informative.
